message:
wa-upg.5.1: storage/indexing perf targets, budgets, and health thresholds

Define measurable storage layer targets as testable Rust types:
- ScaleTargets: 50 panes, 2GB transcripts, 1MB/s ingest, 500K segments
- LatencyBudgets: append p95 <2ms, FTS common <15ms, complex <50ms
- ThroughputBudgets: 500 seg/sec, 128 batch cap, 100 FTS sync batch
- Health thresholds: writer queue, WAL, FTS consistency, indexing lag
- StorageHealthSnapshot::assess() with worst-tier aggregation
- StoragePerfProfile: default/constrained/high_performance presets
- 32 tests covering thresholds, profiles, serde roundtrips

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

 {"id":"wa-upg.1.6","title":"E2E: incident bundle export + replay (verbose logs + artifacts)","description":"# Task: E2E incident bundle export + replay

## Goal
Add an end-to-end scenario that proves incident bundle export and replay works against a real-ish runtime.

## Scenario
- Start a watcher in a controlled test workspace.
- Produce a deterministic incident:
  - example: trigger a known policy denial (AltScreen or recent GAP) OR a known rule match.
- Run `wa reproduce` to export a bundle.
- Run `wa reproduce replay` and assert the expected replay result.

## Requirements
- No fixed sleeps. Use wait-for/quiescence utilities with bounded timeouts.
- Verbose logs:
  - command transcript
  - watcher logs (captured)
  - paths to artifacts
- Artifacts:
  - the generated bundle
  - a small "summary.json" with key assertions

## Acceptance Criteria
- E2E is deterministic and debuggable.
- On failure, artifacts are sufficient to reproduce locally.

 {"id":"wa-upg.1.7","title":"Docs: incident bundles + wa reproduce (shareable support workflow)","description":"# Task: Document incident bundles and `wa reproduce`

## Goal
Make incident bundles discoverable and usable by humans and by support processes.

## Requirements
- Document:
  - when to run `wa reproduce`
  - what data is included by default (and what is not)
  - privacy implications + opt-in flags
  - how to replay a bundle
  - how to attach to a GitHub issue (or share internally)
- Include examples of:
  - a policy denial bundle
  - a rule replay bundle
  - a watcher crash bundle

## Acceptance Criteria
- A new contributor can follow docs to generate and replay a bundle without guesswork.

 {"id":"wa-upg.10","title":"[EPIC] Schema-driven docs + client generation (robot/MCP as real API)","description":"# [EPIC] Schema-driven docs + client generation

## Mission
Treat wa's robot/MCP interfaces as a real API:
- schemas are authoritative
- docs are generated from schemas
- typed clients can be generated or maintained with confidence

## Why This Matters
If an integration breaks silently, users lose trust.
Schemas + generated docs/types provide:
- contract stability
- faster integration development
- safer refactors

## Scope
- Build on the existing JSON Schema work:
  - versioned schemas for robot outputs and error objects
- Add:
  - schema-driven docs pages (render schemas into readable reference)
  - optional client generation (at least one language) OR type-safe Rust client crate
  - CI checks to ensure schema/doc generation stays in sync

## Success Criteria
- Another tool can generate types and call wa robot/MCP reliably.
- Schema changes are intentional and reviewed.

## Testing
- Schema validations in CI.
- Golden tests for generated docs output stability.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Docs reflect schemas automatically.
- At least one typed client path exists and is tested.

-{"id":"wa-upg.10.1","title":"Design: schema→docs/types strategy (direction, versioning, languages)","description":"# Task: Decide schema-driven generation strategy

## Goal
Choose the practical strategy for schema-driven docs and typed clients.

## Requirements
- Decide generation direction:
  - Rust structs → JSON Schema (preferred for single source of truth)
  - OR JSON Schema → types (and keep schemas authored by hand)
- Decide client target(s):
  - TypeScript (common for tooling)
  - Python (common for scripts)
  - Rust client crate (for in-repo safety)
- Decide versioning policy:
  - how schema versions map to wa versions
  - how breaking changes are detected

## Acceptance Criteria
- Strategy is chosen and consistent with repo goals (robust, low tech debt).

 {"id":"wa-upg.10.2","title":"Implement schema-driven docs generator (reference pages)","description":"# Task: Implement schema-driven docs generator

## Goal
Generate readable docs pages from the canonical schemas.

## Requirements
- Generate docs that include:
  - command list
  - request/response objects
  - error objects and codes
  - examples
- Output stability:
  - deterministic ordering
  - stable formatting (golden tests)

## Testing
- Golden tests for generated docs.
- CI check that generated output matches committed files.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Docs pages are usable as an API reference without reading code.
","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","owner":"jeff141421@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-01-18T20:49:04.080280578Z","created_by":"Dicklesworthstone","updated_at":"2026-01-18T20:49:04.080280578Z","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["area-docs","area-robot","area-tests","phase-4"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"wa-upg.10.2","depends_on_id":"wa-upg.10","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-06T04:09:44Z","created_by":"import"}]}
 {"id":"wa-upg.10.3","title":"Typed client generation (at least one language) or Rust client crate","description":"# Task: Typed client generation / client crate

## Goal
Provide a typed client path so integrations are less brittle.

## Requirements
- Choose one practical deliverable:
  - TypeScript types + thin client wrappers
  - Python types + thin client wrappers
  - Rust client crate for wa robot/MCP
- Client must:
  - validate responses against schema (optional but recommended)
  - provide stable error handling (codes)

## Testing
- Integration test that uses the client to call a local wa process and validates schemas.

## Acceptance Criteria
- A consumer can integrate with wa without hand-parsing JSON.

 {"id":"wa-upg.10.4","title":"CI: schema/doc/type generation drift checks","description":"# Task: CI drift checks for schema-driven outputs

## Goal
Ensure schemas, generated docs, and generated types never drift silently.

## Requirements
- CI step that:
  - regenerates docs/types
  - fails if repo has diffs
  - prints actionable diff hints

## Acceptance Criteria
- Breaking schema changes are caught early.

 {"id":"wa-upg.4.6","title":"E2E: pane_uuid remains stable across rename/move","description":"# Task: E2E pane_uuid stability

## Goal
Prove in a real WezTerm session that pane_uuid is stable across common churn.

## Scenario
- Create a pane.
- Ensure pane_uuid assigned.
- Rename pane/title and/or move between tabs.
- Verify:
  - same pane_uuid continues
  - wa continues to attribute output/events correctly

## Requirements
- No fixed sleeps; use wait-for/quiescence.
- Artifacts include:
  - initial pane state
  - post-churn pane state
  - logs showing identity continuity

## Acceptance Criteria
- E2E deterministically validates identity stability.

 {"id":"wa-upg.4.7","title":"Docs: pane_uuid identity model (assignment, stability, debugging)","description":"# Task: Document `pane_uuid`

## Goal
Document the stable pane identity model so humans and agents can:
- understand how `pane_uuid` is assigned
- know what invariants are guaranteed
- debug identity mismatches safely

## Content to include
- Rationale: why `pane_id` is not sufficient as a long-lived identity.
- Strategy chosen:
  - user vars vs OSC handshake vs fingerprint fallback
  - when we write to a pane (if ever), and how we avoid "spam" writes
- Lifecycle:
  - when `pane_uuid` is created
  - how it persists across rename/move
  - what happens on pane recreation
- Storage model:
  - where `pane_uuid` lives in DB
  - how robot/CLI surfaces it
- Debugging guide:
  - how to confirm a pane's identity
  - what evidence is used when identity is uncertain
  - safe remediation steps

## Acceptance Criteria
- A future contributor can reason about pane identity without consulting PLAN.md.
- Docs are explicit about observe/act separation and any write-once handshakes.
","status":"closed","priority":3,"issue_type":"task","owner":"jeff141421@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-01-18T22:11:43.786172711Z","created_by":"Dicklesworthstone","updated_at":"2026-01-23T06:21:49.780042390Z","closed_at":"2026-01-23T06:21:49.779994179Z","close_reason":"Completed: added pane_uuid identity doc section","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["area-docs","area-ux","area-wezterm","phase-1"]}
 {"id":"wa-upg.5","title":"[EPIC] Storage/indexing perf hardening (scale, durability, observability)","description":"# [EPIC] Storage/indexing performance hardening

## Mission
Make storage and indexing robust and fast at scale:
- sustained ingest without lag spikes
- fast FTS queries on large corpora
- predictable compaction/checkpoint behavior
- observable performance (metrics + budgets)

## Why This Matters
wa's DB is its "world memory". If storage is slow or flaky:
- automation becomes laggy
- searches feel useless
- users lose trust

## Scope
This epic is about **hardening** beyond initial correctness:
- indexing progress tracking
- write amplification reduction (batching)
- WAL checkpoint strategy
- FTS incremental sync and vacuum/optimize cadence
- hot-path profiling and budgets

## Success Criteria
- Under stress (large transcripts, many panes), wa stays responsive.
- FTS queries remain fast with stable p95 latency.
- Storage health surfaces make it obvious when indexing is behind.

## Testing
- Benchmarks with budgets for:
  - ingest append
  - FTS query common cases
  - incremental indexing
- Stress E2E:
  - generate large transcript and validate search + no runaway memory

## Acceptance Criteria
- Performance hardening tasks are implemented with measurable improvements.
- Budgets are enforced in CI and regressions are detectable.
","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"epic","owner":"jeff141421@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-01-18T20:40:46.641727420Z","created_by":"Dicklesworthstone","updated_at":"2026-01-26T00:34:53.018006519Z","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["area-perf","area-storage","area-tests","phase-4"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"wa-upg.5","depends_on_id":"wa-upg","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-06T04:09:47Z","created_by":"import"}],"comments":[{"id":215,"issue_id":"wa-upg.5","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"ABSORBED: Now includes wa-3g9.4 (Incremental FTS sync) which was reparented here since FTS indexing performance belongs under storage/indexing hardening, not general performance optimizations.","created_at":"2026-01-26T00:34:53Z"}]}
-{"id":"wa-upg.5.1","title":"Design: storage/indexing perf targets, metrics, and budgets","description":"# Task: Design storage/indexing perf targets

## Goal
Define measurable targets and budgets for storage and indexing.

## Requirements
- Define scale targets:
  - panes: N (e.g., 50+)
  - transcript size: M (e.g., multi-GB)
  - ingest rate: bytes/sec
- Define latency budgets:
  - append segment p95
  - FTS query p95 for common queries
  - indexing lag ceiling
- Define health metrics:
  - writer queue depth
  - WAL size / checkpoint cadence
  - index progress per pane

## Acceptance Criteria
- Targets are explicit and can be enforced by benchmarks and/or E2E.

 {"id":"wa-upg.5.2","title":"Implement indexing progress tracking (per-pane) + health surfaces","description":"# Task: Indexing progress tracking

## Goal
Make indexing performance visible and diagnosable:
- per-pane index progress
- index lag and backfill status

## Requirements
- Track (persist) per-pane indexing progress:
  - last indexed segment seq
  - last indexed timestamp
  - last indexing error (if any)
- Surface via:
  - `wa status` / `wa doctor`
  - robot state

## Testing
- Unit/integration tests:
  - progress updates are monotonic
  - lag computations are correct
  - errors are surfaced with stable codes

## Acceptance Criteria
- Users can tell if search is "behind" and why.
","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","owner":"jeff141421@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-01-18T20:41:10.892779507Z","created_by":"Dicklesworthstone","updated_at":"2026-01-29T04:31:14.941388802Z","closed_at":"2026-01-29T04:31:14.941248271Z","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["area-diagnostics","area-storage","area-tests","phase-4"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"wa-upg.5.2","depends_on_id":"wa-4vx.3.4","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-06T04:09:47Z","created_by":"import"},{"issue_id":"wa-upg.5.2","depends_on_id":"wa-upg.5","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-06T04:09:47Z","created_by":"import"}]}
 {"id":"wa-upg.5.3","title":"Implement write batching + WAL checkpoint/optimize strategy","description":"# Task: Write batching + checkpoint/optimize strategy

## Goal
Reduce write amplification and keep WAL/FTS healthy under sustained ingest.

## Requirements
- Writer batching:
  - batch segment inserts and index updates
  - bounded batch size (time/rows/bytes)
- WAL strategy:
  - periodic checkpoints based on size/time
  - avoid long stalls (do work incrementally)
- FTS maintenance:
  - decide when to run optimize/vacuum-like operations
  - ensure maintenance is explicit and does not surprise users

## Testing
- Integration tests:
  - simulate sustained ingest and assert:
    - WAL does not grow without bound
    - queries remain available
- Benchmarks:
  - compare before/after ingest throughput

## Acceptance Criteria
- Sustained ingest remains stable and storage maintenance is predictable.
","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","assignee":"WildBrook","owner":"jeff141421@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-01-18T20:41:21.359421809Z","created_by":"Dicklesworthstone","updated_at":"2026-01-29T04:11:44.059566699Z","closed_at":"2026-01-29T04:11:44.059438251Z","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["area-perf","area-storage","area-tests","phase-4"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"wa-upg.5.3","depends_on_id":"wa-4vx.3.2","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-06T04:09:47Z","created_by":"import"},{"issue_id":"wa-upg.5.3","depends_on_id":"wa-upg.5","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-06T04:09:47Z","created_by":"import"}]}
 {"id":"wa-upg.5.4","title":"Benchmarks/budgets: storage + FTS p95 regression guards","description":"# Task: Storage/FTS benchmarks and budgets

## Goal
Prevent performance regressions by adding benchmarks and budgets for storage and indexing.

## Requirements
- Criterion benches for:
  - append segments
  - typical FTS queries
  - incremental indexing update step
- Budget reporting:
  - show baseline vs current
  - fail CI on regressions above threshold

## Acceptance Criteria
- Benchmarks run in CI (or in a dedicated perf job) and catch regressions.
